beats.text: Every classifier wants the probability of a class given the features, but there are two very different roads to it.
A discriminative model attacks the posterior directly, learning only where the boundary between classes sits.

visual.note: Modeled head-on by logistic regression, SVMs, neural nets

beats.text: A generative model instead learns how each class produces its data, then flips that with Bayes' rule to recover the posterior.

visual.note: A full story of each class, plus class prevalence

beats.text: Generative classifiers are data-frugal because strong assumptions fill the gaps, while discriminative ones want more data to trace the boundary.
Ng and Jordan showed the generative model reaches its ceiling much faster, winning outright when labeled data is scarce.
With ten examples the model that assumes more usually wins, which is why naive Bayes stays a strong baseline.

concepts.title: Bayes' rule for classification & the naive assumption

beats.text: The engine of naive Bayes is just Bayes' rule, read as a way to score each candidate class for an input.

visual.sub: Prior, likelihood, and the lie that follows

beats.text: Prior times likelihood over evidence gives the posterior, and the evidence is the same for every class, so you can ignore it.

visual.note: EQ M9.1 — the denominator is pure normalization

beats.text: The hard part hides in the likelihood: for fifty binary features the joint needs more parameters than you could ever count.

visual.label: binary features intractable to model jointly

beats.text: So the model makes its defining leap of faith, assuming that given the class, every feature is independent of the others.

visual.note: EQ M9.2 — the cost falls from 2^d parameters to order d

beats.text: Plugging that in and working in log space turns prediction into a single argmax over a sum of log-probabilities.

visual.note: EQ M9.3 — training is counting, inference is addition

beats.text: A prediction is one weighted vote: start from the log-prior, then add each feature's log-evidence for that class.
For continuous features like heights or pixel intensities, each per-feature likelihood is modeled as a one-dimensional Gaussian.

visual.sub: Fit means and variances, then take an argmax

beats.text: The fit is nothing but the sample mean and variance of each feature within each class.

visual.note: EQ M9.4 — one Gaussian per feature per class

beats.text: Because features are assumed independent, each class density is an axis-aligned Gaussian whose contours never tilt.
With shared variances the quadratic terms cancel and the boundary is a straight line, which is Linear Discriminant Analysis.
With per-class variances those terms survive and the boundary bows into a conic, which is Quadratic Discriminant Analysis.
One wrinkle: a near-zero variance makes a Gaussian explode, so production code adds a tiny smoothing term to every variance.

visual.label: variance that must never reach zero

beats.text: Naive Bayes' first and most enduring job was sorting documents, with spam filters making the technique famous.
Multinomial NB treats a document as a bag of word counts, giving each class a vocabulary-sized probability vector.
Each word's probability is its share of the class's tokens, with add-alpha smoothing so an unseen word never zeroes the product.

visual.note: EQ M9.5 — alpha = 1 is Laplace, alpha = 0.5 is Lidstone

beats.text: A worked example: a word seen zero times in a class with six tokens and vocabulary four still earns a smoothed sliver of mass.
Bernoulli NB instead flags each word as present or absent and treats a missing spammy word as evidence for ham.
Bernoulli tends to win on short texts while multinomial wins on longer documents, and both train in a single counting pass.

concepts.title: Smoothing, failure modes & why it works anyway

beats.text: The zero-frequency problem is fatal without smoothing: one unseen word drives its probability to zero and vetoes the whole class.
Read as a Bayesian posterior, alpha is a Dirichlet prior that pre-loads imaginary observations of every word.
When features are correlated, naive Bayes double-counts evidence and grows overconfident, piling posteriors near zero and one.
Here is the paradox: the probabilities are often garbage, yet the classifications are excellent.
Domingos and Pazzani showed the argmax only needs the right class to score highest, not to have an accurate probability.
Robust to irrelevant features and trained in one pass, naive Bayes stays the baseline every other classifier must beat.
